mappings.rationale: Directly covers the civilizations of Mesopotamia, Egypt, and the broader ancient Near East that are the primary subject of this section.

Covers the art and architecture of ancient Egypt, relevant to the section's treatment of Egyptian civilization from the Old Kingdom through the New Kingdom including pyramid building and temple construction.
Directly covers the Old Babylonian Period, the dynasty of Hammurabi, the Kassites, and the Neo-Babylonian Empire under Nebuchadrezzar II, all major topics in this section.
Covers the Assyrian Empire from its early history through its imperial expansion under Tiglath-pileser III, Sargon II, and Sennacherib, all treated extensively in subsections F and G.
The section emphasizes archaeological sources for understanding ancient Near Eastern, Aegean, and North African civilizations, making this foundational VSI directly relevant.
Relevant to the section's coverage of ancient Palestine, the Hebrew kingdoms, the Babylonian Exile, and the archaeological evidence for these biblical-era civilizations.
The section discusses Hebrew culture, the cult of Yahweh, biblical literature, and the Babylonian Exile, all of which intersect with biblical studies.
Relevant to the section's discussion of Hebrew biblical literature, the cult of Yahweh, and the literary sources of the Hebrew kingdom period.
The section covers the Hebrew kingdom, the cult of Yahweh, the Babylonian Exile, and the post-Exile period, all foundational events in the history of Judaism.
Relevant to the section's coverage of the origins of Judaism in the Hebrew kingdoms, the cult of Yahweh, and interactions between Israelite religion and surrounding Near Eastern cultures.
The section explicitly discusses historiographic problems in studying ancient Near Eastern civilizations, making this general introduction to historical method relevant.
The section discusses the invention of cuneiform writing by the Sumerians, the development of the linear alphabet by the Canaanites, and Linear A and B tablets from the Aegean.
Relevant to the section's coverage of Bronze Age Aegean civilizations, the Mycenaean period, and the end of the Bronze Age including destruction of Mycenaean centres.
Directly covers subsection C on the emergence of Egyptian civilization from the Predynastic Period through the Old, Middle, and New Kingdoms.
Relevant to the section's discussion of Egyptian religious and cultural developments, including the cults of Amon-Re and Aton, across the various dynastic periods.
The section covers Iranian history from the Elamites through the Medes, Achaemenid Persians, Parthians, and Sasanians, spanning thousands of years of Iranian civilization.
Relevant to the section's coverage of trade connections between the ancient Near East, Central Asia, and the Mediterranean, especially during the Achaemenid and Parthian periods.
